recognising_ingroup_bias.label: Recognising ingroup bias
recognising_ingroup_bias.guidance: Help the speaker notice that every group, including their own, produces a flattering, self-serving version of events ('here we go again, same story, different country'), so the outgroup's account is not uniquely distorted.

contextualisation.label: Contextualisation
contextualisation.guidance: Place the claim in its socio-political context: when was it said, under what pressures, by whom and for which audience? Restoring context reopens the meaning a loaded frame had closed down.

corroboration.label: Corroboration
corroboration.guidance: Bring in independent third sources to test a claim, rather than accepting or rejecting it on the basis of who said it.

outgroup_empathy.label: Outgroup empathy
outgroup_empathy.guidance: Legitimise the other group's emotional experience and concrete losses ('they too lost homes and family'), so the people behind the alternative view become visible again.

superordinate_identity.label: Superordinate identity
superordinate_identity.guidance: Invite the speaker and audience to see both groups as members of a larger shared category (neighbours, islanders, Europeans, humans) so the us-versus-them frame loses force.

condemnation_of_harm_regardless_of_perpe.label: Condemnation of harm regardless of perpetrator
condemnation_of_harm_regardless_of_perpe.guidance: Condemn wrongdoing whoever commits it, refusing to excuse or amplify harm on the basis of group membership; this separates a stigmatising slur from a principled moral stand.

rigid_opposition.label: Rigid opposition
rigid_opposition.definition: Binary framing that fixes the relation between us and them a priori, demanding total support or total rejection and leaving no middle ground in which rapprochement or dialogue could occur.

examples.text: You are either with us or you are one of them. There is no in-between.
examples.span: either with us or you are one of them
examples.note: Forces a binary identity choice; delegitimises neutrality and dialogue.

examples.text: They will never change, it is in their nature, and we can never live alongside them.
examples.span: never change, it is in their nature
examples.note: Essentialises the outgroup and rules out any future rapprochement.

activities.6-9: Circle game: children name things both communities love (food, games, songs) and draw one shared thing. What do we have in common?
activities.10-13: Venn diagram: where do 'we' and 'they' overlap? Pairs find three shared everyday concerns and present them.
activities.14-17: Rewrite the post so it voices the same worry without the either/or frame; compare how each version makes a reader feel.
activities.18+: Analyse the post with the common ingroup identity model and draft a bridge formulation that invokes a shared superordinate category.

transfer_of_meaning.label: Transfer of meaning
transfer_of_meaning.definition: Linking the target to an already richly loaded and hostile category (a foreign power, an enemy ideology, or a contamination/invasion frame) so that the established emotion attaches automatically and no fresh evidence needs to be weighed. This includes dehumanising metaphors that import pre-loaded fear or disgust (flooding, disease, infestation) and conspiracy frames that import pre-loaded hostility toward foreign enemies.

examples.text: These NGOs are just agents of foreign powers pushing their plan on our island.
examples.note: Transfers pre-loaded hostility toward foreign interference onto local civic actors so their arguments need no examination.

examples.text: Anyone calling for reconciliation is a tool of the same outsiders who divided us.
examples.note: Re-categorises peace advocates under an enemy frame, importing wholesale distrust.

examples.text: Migrants are flooding our schools and bringing disease. They do not belong here.
examples.span: flooding our schools and bringing disease
examples.note: Flood and disease metaphors transfer pre-loaded fear and disgust onto a group, bypassing any factual claim.

activities.6-9: Sorting game: show that a label ('the bad guys') does not tell us what a real person did today; ask what we would need to find out first.
activities.10-13: Spot-the-shortcut: students underline the loaded label in the post and write the actual claim it skips over.
activities.14-17: Trace the transfer: identify the loaded category invoked, then research whether it actually fits the person or group being described.
activities.18+: Map the representational chain from core opposition to secondary target and write a contextualising rebuttal that re-separates them.

prohibited_thoughts.label: Prohibited thoughts
prohibited_thoughts.definition: Marking the alternative representation as so dangerous that merely entertaining it invites catastrophe, using fear to prevent people from elaborating or even considering the other view.

examples.text: Even talking about a shared future is the end of our community; it would be suicidal to consider it.
examples.note: Frames mere discussion as an existential threat to deter any engagement with the alternative.

examples.text: To listen to their version is to betray our dead, and there is no future down that road.
examples.note: Uses fear and taboo so the alternative is sealed off from elaboration.

activities.6-9: Brave-question time: it is safe to ask questions and wonder; practise saying 'I would like to find out more' about something unfamiliar.
activities.10-13: Separate the feeling from the fact: students name the fear in the post, then list one thing they could safely check or ask about.
activities.14-17: Examine the taboo: what makes this topic 'forbidden', and does discussing it actually cause the harm claimed? Test with a counter-example.
activities.18+: Analyse the prohibition as a promoter sign (Valsiner); design a safe deliberative format that lets the alternative be examined without threat.

stigma.label: Stigma
stigma.definition: Stereotyping or demeaning the people who hold the alternative view so that their position becomes socially unacceptable and ingroup members fear losing standing if they engage with it.

examples.text: Anyone who wants to cooperate with them is a naive sell-out and not a real patriot.
examples.note: Demeans members of one's own group who engage the alternative, enforcing conformity through shame.

examples.text: People who attend those joint events are just brainwashed traitors.
examples.note: Stigmatises participants so others avoid even being seen near the alternative view.

activities.6-9: Kind-words swap: notice a name that puts someone down and practise describing the person by what they actually did instead.
activities.10-13: Name-the-move: students identify the put-down in the post and rewrite the sentence describing the person fairly.
activities.14-17: Discuss the social cost: how does the slur police the ingroup? Role-play standing up for a fair description without endorsing any harm.
activities.18+: Analyse stigma as a structure of recognition within the ingroup; draft a response that condemns real harm while refusing the dehumanising label.

undermining_motive.label: Undermining the motive of the author
undermining_motive.definition: Attributing self-interested or hidden motives to whoever holds the alternative view, providing a ready ad hominem reason to dismiss what they say without engaging its substance.

examples.text: The researchers behind that report are just paid by outside donors with a hidden agenda.
examples.span: paid by outside donors with a hidden agenda
examples.note: Dismisses the content by impugning motives rather than weighing the evidence.

examples.text: Of course they call for dialogue, they only want the funding that comes with it.
examples.note: Provides a widely applicable ad hominem to discount the alternative.

activities.6-9: Check-it game: when someone says 'they only said that to be mean', practise asking 'how could we find out if it is true?'
activities.10-13: Argument vs author: students separate the claim from the attack on the speaker, then judge the claim on its own.
activities.14-17: Spot the ad hominem: identify the motive attack, then evaluate the underlying claim using an independent source.
activities.18+: Distinguish legitimate interest disclosure from motive-poisoning; corroborate the claim through third sources and weigh it on the merits.

distrust.label: Distrust
distrust.definition: Dismissing the alternative on the basis of categorical, history-laden distrust of its source group, so that engaging the other side at all is framed as naive or foolish.

examples.text: How can you trust anything they say? History shows they always go back on their word.
examples.span: How can you trust anything they say
examples.note: Generalises distrust to the whole source group, making engagement seem foolish.

examples.text: Never trust the other side, it always ends the same way.
examples.note: Categorical distrust forecloses any dialogue or verification.

activities.6-9: Trust-step story: read about two children who slowly learn to trust by keeping small promises; what helped them?
activities.10-13: Evidence over reputation: students find one verifiable fact in the post and check it against another source before judging.
activities.14-17: Distinguish a specific broken promise from a blanket judgement of a whole group; discuss how trust can be rebuilt in steps.
activities.18+: Examine threat-based representations and non-dialogue (Avraamidou & Psaltis); design a corroboration protocol for contested claims.

bracketing.label: Bracketing
bracketing.definition: Holding the alternative representation at a linguistic distance, through reported speech and hedging ('they claim', 'so-called'), through outright refusal to engage ('their version', 'nothing to discuss'), or through dismissal that prevents the alternative from ever being examined as a genuine view.

examples.text: Their so-called peace plan supposedly benefits everyone, or so they claim.
examples.note: Triple bracketing holds the alternative in suspended disbelief without engaging it.

examples.text: They say they want reconciliation, allegedly, but who really knows.
examples.note: Reported speech and hedging keep the alternative attributed to others and unreal.

examples.text: Do not even discuss their version of history in class, there is nothing to discuss.
examples.note: Flags the alternative view as unworthy of examination, foreclosing engagement without engaging the content.

activities.6-9: Tell-it-straight game: practise saying what someone actually wants in plain words instead of 'they say maybe sort of'.
activities.10-13: Un-bracket the sentence: students rewrite the post stating the other side's view directly, then notice how it reads differently.
activities.14-17: Hunt the hedges: highlight every distancing word and discuss what work each one does to keep the alternative unreal.
activities.18+: Analyse the bracketing as management of representational multiplicity; rewrite to engage the strongest version of the alternative.
